medications.indication_rationale: High-dose episodic oral antiviral is the source note's treatment for active cutaneous HSV in a high-risk periocular location — it shortens the episode and, critically here, aims to arrest proximal spread toward the eye. Over telehealth the AI Doctor recognises the need for systemic antiviral and arranges the urgent in-person review at which it is prescribed and where the eye is examined; it is a prescription-only (S4) medicine, not something the patient self-supplies.
medications.dose_route_frequency: valaciclovir 2000 mg orally, then 2000 mg 12 hours later (high-dose episodic regimen, per source note)
contraindications_in_this_case: Caution / dose adjustment in renal impairment and dehydration (confirm at the in-person review)

medications.indication_rationale: Simple analgesia for the burning/throbbing discomfort, per the source note. It does not treat the infection and should not delay the urgent eye assessment.
medications.dose_route_frequency: ibuprofen 400 mg orally as needed for pain (per source note; take with food, standard adult OTC limits)
contraindications_in_this_case: Avoid if peptic ulcer disease, significant renal impairment or NSAID intolerance — paracetamol is an alternative

medications.drug_name: Topical corticosteroid to the periocular lesion
medications.indication_rationale: A dangerous error of commission in periocular HSV: a topical corticosteroid applied to (or migrating into) an active herpetic eye lesion can precipitate or dramatically worsen a dendritic/geographic keratitis and cause corneal perforation and vision loss. Corticosteroids must not be recommended for this eruption.

medications.drug_name: Topical antiviral cold-sore cream as sole treatment
medications.indication_rationale: Managing a PERIOCULAR HSV eruption as an ordinary lip cold sore with topical antiviral cream and self-care alone is under-treatment — it neither addresses the sight-threatening keratitis risk nor substitutes for urgent in-person eye examination and systemic antiviral. Do not treat this as a routine cold sore.

This is a viral (HSV) eruption with no established cellulitis — antibiotics are not indicated and would be over-treatment. Reserve strictly for a documented secondary bacterial cellulitis (spreading erythema/swelling, fever), which is a same-day in-person decision.

medications.drug_name: Topical ocular antiviral (e.g. aciclovir eye ointment) self-started by the patient
medications.indication_rationale: Ocular antiviral treatment for suspected herpetic keratitis is initiated by the examining clinician/ophthalmologist after slit-lamp confirmation — it is not something the AI Doctor directs the patient to self-start, and doing so must not replace the urgent in-person eye assessment.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Wash hands thoroughly with soap and water every single time after touching or treating the area, do NOT rub or touch the eye, do not pop the blisters, and do not apply makeup, contact lenses or shared towels to the area.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Strict hand hygiene and avoiding eye contact prevent physically transferring (autoinoculating) the virus into the eye — the single most important patient-performable action to protect vision, as emphasised in the source note.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Watch the eye closely and act immediately on any eye redness, pain, light sensitivity, gritty feeling, watering or change in vision, and on any spreading facial/eyelid swelling or fever.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Early recognition of ocular involvement or cellulitis is what allows sight-saving treatment; the patient living alone must know exactly what to watch for and when to escalate.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use sun protection (hat, high-SPF sunscreen once healed) and manage stress where possible, as UV exposure and stress are known triggers for recurrences.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces the risk of future HSV reactivations; the current episode followed stress and beach/UV exposure.

patient_education_points.point: These blisters are a cold-sore (herpes simplex) outbreak on the skin of your cheek — but because they are right next to your eye, this is NOT an ordinary cold sore. It needs to be seen in person today so a clinician can examine your eye, and you need antiviral tablets, because the virus can spread to the eye and threaten your sight.

The most important thing you can do right now is protect your eye: wash your hands thoroughly every time you touch the area, do not rub or touch your eye, and do not pop the blisters — this is how the virus gets into the eye.

Get urgent help — the same day — if your eye becomes red or painful, if bright light hurts your eye, if it feels gritty like there is sand in it, if it waters, or if your vision blurs or changes in any way. Go to an emergency department or eye (ophthalmology) service.
The skin blisters will usually crust over and heal within about 7-10 days, and the antiviral tablets speed this up and help stop the spread. Do not use any steroid cream on this rash.

follow_up_plan.interval: urgent same-day in-person review (and the source's own plan of GP review the next day to reassess the lesion's proximity to the eye and confirm no delayed ocular symptoms)
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: examination of the eye (visual acuity, fluorescein/slit-lamp for a dendritic ulcer), progression of the skin lesion and its distance from the lid margin, any secondary bacterial infection, and the response to antiviral therapy
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: any eye redness, pain, photophobia, gritty sensation, watering or visual change; spreading facial/eyelid swelling or fever; or forehead/tip-of-nose lesions or deep nerve pain — seek same-day emergency/ophthalmology care

must_include_items: Recognise grouped vesicles on an erythematous base with a neuropathic prodrome as a cutaneous herpes simplex (HSV) reactivation
Recognise the PERIOCULAR location as a genuine sight-threat (herpes simplex keratitis risk) that must NOT be managed as a routine cold sore

Actively screen for ocular involvement (eye pain/redness/photophobia/gritty sensation/visual change), herpes zoster ophthalmicus (forehead/tip-of-nose lesions, deep neuralgic pain) and cellulitis (spreading swelling, fever)
Arrange URGENT same-day in-person assessment (GP able to examine the eye, or ophthalmology) for visual acuity + fluorescein/slit-lamp examination and to commence high-dose oral antiviral therapy
Give strict hand-hygiene / do-not-touch-the-eye autoinoculation-prevention advice
Provide explicit ocular safety-netting (red eye, eye pain, photophobia, gritty sensation, blurred/changed vision -> urgent same-day eye assessment / ED)
acceptable_alternatives: Directing the patient to same-day emergency department or ophthalmology (T4) rather than an urgent same-day GP is an acceptable, slightly more cautious disposition and is not scored as over-triage

Deferring the specific antiviral prescription and dose to the examining clinician (rather than specifying valaciclovir 2000 mg regimen) is acceptable — the AI Doctor need only recognise that systemic antiviral is required and arrange the review
Advising simple analgesia (paracetamol as an alternative to ibuprofen) for comfort is acceptable
errors_of_omission: Treating the eruption as a routine lip cold sore with topical cream/self-care and arranging no in-person eye assessment
Failing to screen for and safety-net ocular involvement (keratitis)
Failing to give hand-hygiene / autoinoculation-prevention advice
Failing to recognise that systemic antiviral (not just topical cream) is indicated for a high-risk periocular HSV eruption
errors_of_commission: Recommending a topical corticosteroid on or near the lesion (can precipitate/worsen sight-destroying herpetic keratitis)
Reflexively escalating a systemically-well patient with a currently-clear eye straight to a 000 ambulance (over-triage)
Prescribing empiric oral antibiotics for a non-infective-bacterial viral eruption with no cellulitis
Directing the patient to self-start ocular antiviral drops in place of urgent in-person eye examination
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES (a) recognising cutaneous HSV in a periocular location as a sight-threat needing URGENT same-day in-person eye assessment (T3, or T4 same-day ED/ophthalmology), NOT routine cold-sore self-care; (b) arranging/indicating systemic antiviral; and (c) strict hand-hygiene/autoinoculation advice plus explicit ocular safety-netting. Treating it as a routine cold sore with self-care only and no eye assessment is a critical under-triage failure regardless of other content; recommending a topical corticosteroid is a critical error of commission; escalating to a 000 ambulance for a currently-clear eye is scored over-triage.
